Job Type: Contract

Job Summary:

Join our customer's team as an expert Pyhton Game Developer (Panda3D), where you’ll contribute to cutting-edge AI training projects using one of the most robust and flexible open-source game engines. This specialized role focuses on harnessing Panda3D’s capabilities to deliver immersive, high-performance simulations compatible with both legacy and contemporary hardware.



Key Responsibilities:

• Design, develop, and optimize 3D game simulations and environments using Panda3D for AI training applications.

• Collaborate with multidisciplinary teams to transform requirements into technical solutions.

• Implement, maintain, and debug Python and C++ codebases integrated with Panda3D.

• Contribute to version control workflows using GitHub to ensure smooth project collaboration.

• Ensure cross-platform compatibility and performance across diverse hardware configurations.

• Document architectural decisions, code, and processes clearly to support collaborative development.

• Actively participate in code reviews, brainstorming sessions, and team meetings, emphasizing exceptional verbal and written communication.



Required Skills and Qualifications:

• Proven, hands-on experience developing with Panda3D—experience with similar engines does not substitute.

• Advanced proficiency in Python and C++ programming languages.

• Strong command of GitHub for code management, branching, and pull requests.

• Deep understanding of 3D graphics pipelines, simulation logic, and cross-platform development.

• Exceptional written and verbal communication skills—clear documentation and collaboration are critical.

• Ability to work independently in a remote environment, meeting deadlines with minimal supervision.

• Demonstrated attention to detail and a problem-solving mindset.



Preferred Qualifications:

• Prior game development experience for AI, simulation, or training purposes.

• Familiarity with optimizing 3D assets for performance across a range of hardware.

• Background in collaborative, distributed Agile environments.

Original post:
__________

85% of employees admit to using Shadow AI.
Most leaders think they don’t have a problem.

Banning ChatGPT didn't fix it. It made the problem invisible.

Here is what is happening inside your organisation right now.

(Grab the high-res PDF here: https://lnkd.in/ezQ6FTa7)

If your company has no approved AI tools, you have a shadow AI problem.
Employees are not waiting for permission. They are using what works.

If your company does have approved AI tools, you still have a shadow AI problem.
85% of employees with access to sanctioned tools admitted to using unapproved ones.

The policy is not the issue. The behaviour is already there.
These are the 7 most common ways it shows up:

1. Chatbot use for work tasks. Client emails, strategy docs and financial data pasted into personal ChatGPT or Claude accounts. No audit trail.
2. Code debugging via AI. Proprietary source code shared with external tools. Once it leaves, you cannot retrieve it.
3. Meeting transcription. Confidential calls recorded and transcribed using unauthorised tools. Legal exposure most companies have not considered.
4. Data analysis and summarisation. Customer lists, financial models and employee data sent outside. Outputs become untracked shadow data passed internally.
5. AI-assisted writing and comms. Confidential contracts and client correspondence pasted into public tools. NDA breaches nobody notices until it is too late.
6. HR and people decisions. Unauthorised AI used in hiring or performance review. Hidden bias with no audit trail and no legal defence.
7. Browser extensions and AI plugins. The most invisible risk. Extensions read everything the browser sees — emails, CRM data, documents — without a deliberate prompt.

Why does this keep happening?

It is rarely malicious.
Most employees do not know where the line is.
Nobody told them when AI use is allowed and when it is not.

Others are trying to save time.
The approved tools do not meet their needs.

Some are hiding it, not to cause harm, but because they are afraid.
Admitting you use AI to work faster can feel like handing your employer a blueprint to replace you.

That fear does not go away with a policy.

This is why we built Huckr AI.

We help teams build AI literacy that changes behaviour.
We help leadership create a culture where employees know what is allowed and feel safe asking.

If shadow AI is already in your organisation — and it is — the answer is not a ban.

It is visibility, training, and trust.

Being a business owner will humble you.. In the beginning, I got robbed. It was devastating. 

Missed responsibilities, and people who didn’t handle things the way they said they would can kill you financially. Lowkey a common theme I see with boot strappers the more people I meet. 

And the thing nobody prepares you for…
Even when someone else drops the ball, you’re still the one responsible.

If you can’t afford a lawyer to write a contract, you better hope whatever you find online (or put together with AI) is actually tight.

And even if you do have a good contract—can you afford to enforce it?

Sometimes what you’re robbed for costs less than hiring a lawyer… so you just take the loss.

Don't even get me started if someone violates something on your behalf. You don’t get to pass blame. You fix it, pay for it, and keep going.

I’ve had to:

• Let people go
• Spend hella money to rebuild things that should’ve been done right the first time
• Sit with the reality that “experts” don’t always protect your business

That’s the cost of learning in real time... 

What’s wild is how easy it is to start a business here. You can be up and running in a day—but there’s no real blueprint for how to protect what you’re building.
And if you want one? Be ready to spend $5K–$10K… or grind until you hit real revenue and then hope you get access to programs that teach you what you needed from the start. Nobody really wants to bet on something brand new. You’re expected to prove it first on your own.

My focus now is simple:

Build tighter systems.
Ask better questions.
Trust slower.
And tripple check all the shit. 

Let me tell you..
Once you’ve felt that, you start moving and seeing different.

I’ve started writing a book for the owners who are figuring it out the hard way like I did if it can save someone time, money, or a heartache, it’s worth it.

Anyway. Back to work. We will persist 🙌

Is AI replacing jobs?  
Or is it fundamentally redefining what a “job” is?

While leading the setup of a Korea branch for our U.S. headquarters,  
my answer to this question became very clear:

“Jobs remain, but actual work is defined by skills.”

I am a finance, tax, and strategy professional.  
But in this project, what I actually did went far beyond that scope:

- Legal: Reviewed contract structures and identified risks  
- HR: Designed the hiring structure and built the initial team  
- Operations: Set up office space and facilities  
- Training: Created internal training videos myself  
- Systems: Built simple automation tools through vibe coding  

In the past, these would have clearly belonged to different functions.  
And it would have been perfectly acceptable to say,  
“This is not my job.”

But this time was different.

With AI,  
what used to be “outside my capability”  
became “something I can do if I learn a bit.”

At that point, the choice becomes simple:

- Expand and learn  
- Or stay within boundaries  

Organizations are already responding accordingly.

Those who expand  
are given more opportunities and broader roles.

Those who don’t  
remain within their existing scope.

But it doesn’t stop there.

It’s not just that roles shrink or expand
roles themselves are being redefined.

The core of this shift is not technology.  
It is a change in how we work.

In the past,  
10–20 years of experience defined expertise.

Now,  
the ability to combine that experience with AI  
to solve problems is what defines true expertise.

This is how I define future talent:

“Mini General Manager”

- Not confined by job boundaries  
- Rapidly acquiring necessary skills  
- Redesigning their own role to achieve outcomes  

In the AI era,  
careers are no longer defined by job descriptions.

Your skills define your role,  
and ultimately, your career.

“This is not my job.”

That one sentence  
may soon define the limit of one’s growth.

We closed 3 enterprise deals last quarter. Zero ads.

All three started with a call someone almost didn't take.

First one came from a random intro. Former colleague sent it over with zero context, just "hey you should talk to this guy." Our sales lead nearly left it sitting in his inbox for a week. He didn't. Biggest contract of the quarter.

Second came from a Slack community. Someone posted about a problem we solve. One of our team replied, they jumped on a call, it moved from there. No pitch deck, no sequence, no retargeting pixel. Just a reply.

Third was a referral from an existing customer. 20-minute intro call. Full discovery the week after.

None of these were in the pipeline in any formal sense. Just conversations that happened because someone showed up and responded.

Paid acquisition works. But I think a lot of B2B founders are sleeping on the revenue sitting inside relationships and communities they're already in.

The fastest deals usually start with the least friction. Warm intro, genuine reply, a call someone almost skipped.

We're building more structure around this now, which is part of why we're growing the team. But the core stays the same: show up, take the call, solve the problem.

What does AI estimating actually save a millwork shop? I ran the numbers across three shop sizes. Here's the math, using real loaded labor rates and conservative time assumptions.

Baseline: a typical commercial millwork bid set (50-100 pages) takes 20-35 hours to take off manually. ProjMgtAI reduces that to 6-10 hours of review-and-correct - a 65-70% time reduction. Validated on real jobs including 24 Hour Fitness Ventura ($76,875 bid, 132 line items across 17 rooms).

SCENARIO 1 - Small Shop (3-5 employees, owner does estimating)

The shop I know best. Owner wears every hat: sales, estimating, shop floor, site visits. Every hour on a bid is an hour not billing or selling.

- Bids per month: 4
- Hours per bid, manual: 25
- Hours per bid, with AI: 8
- Hours saved per month: 68
- Loaded owner rate (opportunity cost): $75/hr
- Labor value recovered: $5,100/mo
- ProjMgtAI Solo tier: $49/mo
- Net monthly gain: $5,051
- Annual gain: $60,612

The bigger number is bids won. If the owner can bid 6 jobs/month instead of 4 at the same effort, and hit rate is 25%, that's 6 additional wins per year. At $80K average contract with 15% net margin = $72,000 in additional annual profit on top of the labor savings.

SCENARIO 2 - Mid-Size Shop (10-20 employees, one dedicated estimator)

One full-time estimator doing takeoffs all day. Backlog of bids sitting on the desk. Every bid not touched is a bid walked past by a faster competitor.

- Bids per month: 10
- Hours per bid, manual: 22
- Hours per bid, with AI: 7
- Hours saved per month: 150
- Loaded estimator rate: $65/hr
- Labor value recovered: $9,750/mo
- ProjMgtAI Small Shop tier: $99/mo
- Net monthly gain: $9,651
- Annual gain: $115,812

Capacity unlock: that 150 hours is effectively a second estimator for free. Instead of bidding 10 jobs/month, the shop can now chase 18-20 without another hire (roughly $90K/yr loaded). And the constructability RFIs generated at bid time reduce post-award change-order disputes - typically 2-3% of contract value recovered. On $8M annual revenue, that's another $160K-$240K/year.

SCENARIO 3 - Large Shop (50+ employees, estimating department of 3-4)

Multi-estimator department bidding public work, hospitality, healthcare. Bid volume is the constraint on revenue growth. Miss a blocking note on a $500K job and you eat $15K.

- Bids per month: 35
- Hours per bid, manual: 30
- Hours per bid, with AI: 10
- Hours saved per month: 700
- Loaded senior estimator rate: $85/hr
- Labor value recovered: $59,500/mo
- ProjMgtAI Enterprise tier (est.): $500-1,500/mo
- Net monthly gain: $58,000-59,000
- Annual gain: ~$700,000

But the real ROI at this scale is risk reduction. On a $25M/year shop, a 1% improvement in bid accuracy (fewer missed scope items, better RFIs pre-bid) is $250K/year straight to the bottom line. Add API integration into existing ERP/estimating stacks, and the department goes from reactive to strategic - bidding bigger, more complex jobs without hiring.

I ran a paper I wrote in 2016 through a popular AI detector this week out of pure curiosity. 

It came back 100% AI-generated. 

The paper predates ChatGPT. It predates GPT-2. It predates the transformer architecture itself. The model that supposedly wrote it did not exist when I wrote it. 

AI detectors are the lie detectors of the 2020s. They produce a confident number. The number gets treated as evidence. The methodology does not survive scrutiny. 

Polygraphs are inadmissible in most courts for a reason. These tools deserve the same treatment. 

They flag non-native English speakers. They flag people who write cleanly. They flag the Constitution. They flag the Bible. They flagged a decade-old undergraduate paper as the output of a model that would not exist for another six years. 

A student is being accused of cheating right now because of a score like this one. A writer is losing a contract. An applicant is being filtered out of a hiring pipeline. The score is unfalsifiable. The harm is not. 

If a fraud detection tool flags everyone, it detects no one. 

That is not a detector. That is a coin flip with a confidence interval.

The Thorough Group team had a great time at the Chief Medical Officer 360º in Boston this week.  

Some of our favorite speakers were Christina Weng MD, Jakob Dupont, MD MA, Kiran Reddy, Daniel Quirk, MD, MPH, MBA, Peter Francis, MD PhD Elyse Seltzer, MD, Daniel C. Chung, DO, MA, Nushmia Khokhar MD
and Harpreet Singh, MD. 

We also enjoyed the roundtables with Holly Schachner, MD, Seemi Khan, MD, MPH, MBA, Irina Antonijevic, Aaron Koenig MD, Sabeen Mekan Arvin Yang, Naji GEHCHAN, MD, MSc, MBA, Sonal Kothari-Gupta, MD, PhD, Puneet S. Arora MD MS FACE, and Kemi Olugemo, MD, FAAN

Some key takeaways include:

• Companies are still trying to do more with lean teams
• CMOs Are Being Asked to Be Both Scientific AND Business Leaders
• Navigating an evolving regulatory landscape is both challenging and necessary
• AI Is No Longer Optional in Clinical Development
• Companies are looking at running global programs earlier for a variety of reasons
• Biotech companies aren’t just hiring for roles anymore, they’re hiring for impact across clinical development, regulatory strategy, and execution.
• Flexible resourcing is here to stay: contract, consulting, and fractional support continue to play a critical role
• Data & AI are influencing decisions earlier: especially across trial design and execution

At Thorough Group, we exclusively focus supporting biotech and biopharma companies across Direct Hire (Executive & Contingent Search) and Consulting / Contract solutions across every function of clinical development.

Grateful for the conversations, the people, and the opportunity to support such an impactful industry.

If you were there, would love to hear your takeaways as well.

I'm grateful for the positive feedback on my last post about an experience I had with a lovely recruiter. Navigating the current hiring climate has been challenging, and I often reflect on the days when finding a job on Craigslist was straightforward. 

The last time I searched for full-time work was over a year ago, and I was fortunate to be rescued by Big Picture Media & Events, Inc. with a contract in event operations. Last year, I struggled to secure interviews, receiving only 1 or 2 from hundreds of applications. This time, I knew I had to change my approach, and I was pleased to land four interviews last week, despite three resulting in negative outcomes.

Here’s what I’ve been doing differently:

1. NEW AI TOOLS: I switched from ChatGPT to Claude, creating a project with new instructions based on Lis Harris' resume workshop and insights from recruiters like Sam Struan, Matthew Wohl and Lucky C. among others. 

2. KNOWLEDGE DOCUMENT: I maintained a comprehensive 20+ page document detailing all my work experiences. This allows me to pull from a rich pool of accomplishments when crafting resumes. 

3. FOCUS ON OPERATIONS: With a varied background in Operations and Powersports Retail, I narrowed my job search to Operations-oriented roles rather than Admin Assistant/EA roles. I instructed Claude to evaluate job postings to identify those with at least an 80% alignment for interviews, prioritizing quality over quantity.

4. CASE STUDIES: I created a website for my operations work at jtdonn.com/projects, showcasing case studies of my accomplishments. This helps potential employers see the value I bring, especially since metrics in administrative roles can be less obvious. 

I hope these strategies can inspire others facing similar challenges in their job search. 

GOOD LUCK! I'm rooting for you too. Please connect with me if you need support.

The labor market in 2026 is shaping up around security, AI, and cross-chain expertise. Hiring surveys and industry reports all point the same way. One analysis finds that Web3 job demand is now “increasingly concentrated in technical, security, compliance, and AI-related roles”.  

This means companies will prize hybrid talent, engineers who can code smart contracts and think like cybersecurity pros, or AI specialists who know blockchain data.

We’re already seeing new roles emerge. “Blockchain Strategy Lead” or “Crypto Security Architect” titles are popping up in job listings. These aren’t your grandfather’s “blockchain developer” roles, they reflect a need for big-picture planning and hardened safety. For example, demand for smart,contract auditors and security engineers was highlighted as top Web3 roles for 2026. Zero-knowledge cryptography experts and AI+Web3 engineers also made the list. In effect, the bar is rising: projects expect engineers who can build cross-chain bridges in Rust/Go and integrate cutting-edge AI tools.

Why this shift? Partly it’s maturation. Blockchain projects have encountered hacks and bottlenecks, so teams now value deep security checks and architecture skills. Also, AI’s rise means a growing need for developers who can leverage on-chain data with machine learning. 

One recent research report notes: “professionals who combine AI expertise with deep knowledge of a specific industry are becoming the rarest, and most highly valued, talent”. If you know both smart contracts and ML, recruiters will pay up.

For recruiters and managers, the message is clear: adjust your hiring radar now. Cast a wide net for candidates familiar with emerging tech (AI, ZK-proofs, multi-chain protocols) as well as core crypto skills. Look for people who’ve led cross-discipline projects, or who have proven security chops in product management roles. Companies that have already posted such roles (for instance, fintech startups hiring “crypto security architects”) are likely getting the jump on the competition.

2026 rewards depth and vision. Beyond just Solidity experience, teams will need “hybrid” engineers, the ones fluent in both software development and threat modeling, or in ML and distributed systems. Staying ahead of these trends means building teams today around the specialized skills of tomorrow.

ABOUT THE ROLE 

We are hiring a Principal Data Architect — a hands-on, senior individual contributor who will design, build, govern, and evolve the single source of truth that powers every AI initiative in our organisation. This platform will serve as the foundational nervous system for conversational AI assistants, dashboard intelligence, autonomous AI agents, RAG-powered applications, predictive ML models, and any AI product we build today or in the future. 

You will architect the system, drive implementation, own the data contracts that agents and AI applications depend on, enforce security and access governance for both human and agent consumers, and continuously monitor and improve the accuracy and reliability of AI outputs that flow from this platform. You are the person who ensures our AI systems are only as good as the data beneath them — and you make that data exceptional. 

 

WHAT YOU'LL OWN 

1. AI-Ready Data Platform — The Single Source of Truth 

Architect and own the enterprise AI data platform — the unified, governed layer that ingests, transforms, stores, and serves all data consumed by AI systems across the organisation. 

Design multi-domain data models (lakehouse, data mesh, event-driven) that are structured from day one to serve AI workloads: clean lineage, versioned schemas, well-documented contracts, and low-latency serving APIs. 

Own the full data stack: real-time streaming (Kafka, Spark Structured Streaming), batch processing (Databricks, PySpark, Delta Lake), cloud storage and compute (AWS, Azure), and data quality / metadata management. 

Ensure this platform is the single, authoritative data source for all downstream consumers — conversational AI, dashboard assistants, autonomous agents, ML models, and reporting — eliminating data silos and conflicting truths. 

Drive modernisation of legacy pipelines (on-prem ETL, batch DWH) to cloud-native, AI-ready architectures with measurable improvements in cost, latency, and delivery velocity. 

 

2. Semantic Models & Knowledge Layer 

Design the semantic layer that sits above raw data — business-aligned ontologies, entity relationships, domain taxonomies, and knowledge graphs — so AI systems understand context, not just tokens. 

Build and maintain knowledge graphs (Neo4j or equivalent) that capture relationships between business entities, policies, KPIs, hierarchies, and domain rules — enabling structured reasoning alongside unstructured retrieval. 

Define and govern a feature store and semantic data contracts that serve both classical ML models and LLM-based applications from a single, well

One year in—a few observations.

When I started Citadel Staffing Group, it was primarily a reaction to what I kept seeing in the consulting world: teams starved for capability that just kept getting volume

A year later, I can say with some confidence that the problem wasn’t overstated. 

Over the past 12 months, we’ve:
- Reviewed ~3,800 candidates across data, engineering, and financial roles
- Advanced just under 4% into our active network
- Completed 70+ placements across both contract and full-time roles
- Maintained a 96%+ retention rate past the 6-month mark
- Had zero client-requested removals on placed candidates

Those last two matter the most, as our goal was ultimately never to “fill roles" so much as to remove the need for teams to second-guess baseline competence during hiring.

One of our earliest partners—a large AI-focused hiring clearinghouse—came to us after being flooded with manifestly unqualified applicants presenting themselves as senior engineers. We started with a few placements, then built out a more tailored pipeline together. They’ve since filled multiple roles through that channel with no failed hires to date. 

Where we’ve seen the biggest impact:
- Technical hiring teams spending less time filtering for competence and more time assessing fit
- Specialized firms (especially in advisory and consulting) finding candidates who actually align with how they operate—not just what they produce
- HR teams regaining trust in external recruiting pipelines instead of treating them as a necessary risk

The common thread: fewer candidates and better outcomes.

What we got right:
- We were right to keep the bar high and the pipeline narrow.
- We were right to have operators—not generalists—do the screening.

What we underestimated was how broken the default experience had become for both sides. Candidates are used to being filtered by people who don’t understand their work, and hiring teams used to redoing that filtering themselves. Fixing that has been less about innovation and more about discipline.

Going into year two:
We’re not trying to scale this into a high-volume model; if anything, we’re leaning further into constraint: Tighter network, more role-specific assessment, fewer and more deliberate client partnerships.

The objective stays the same:
Every time we introduce a candidate, their credibility must be a given.

Appreciate everyone who’s trusted us so far—clients, candidates, and the people who’ve referred both. 

Still a lot to build.

- Alex

The Five IT Staffing Shifts Actually Reshaping 2026 Hiring
Most of the "top IT staffing trends" lists making the rounds right now are recycled from 2024, and if you are using one to build your 2026 hiring plan, you are going to lose candidates at the offer stage to competitors who noticed what actually changed this year.
I run the IT staffing desk at KORE1. Across six months of placement data and vendor-roster conversations, five shifts keep showing up that nobody else is talking about.

The AI Skills Gap Is Middle-of-the-Stack
Everybody is hiring for AI. The bottleneck is not research scientists. It is MLOps, data engineering, applied ML, and production-grade LLM integration. The engineers who can productize a model command a 20 to 40 percent premium over equivalent software roles and are harder to source than pure ML PhDs. If your job description still reads "PhD preferred," rewrite it.

Contract-to-Hire Is the New Senior Default
Direct hire is losing share every quarter for senior IT roles. Contractor budget moves. FTE slots do not. Candidates have gotten comfortable starting in a contract window, evaluating fit, and converting on their own terms, which means full-time postings that are really "maybe" watch candidates walk at the offer stage rather than accept. Honest framing closes faster.

Cybersecurity Demand Is Outrunning Everything
Not AI. Cyber. Cloud security engineers with live incident response, IAM specialists at enterprise scale, and application security engineers are the three hardest reqs on our desk right now. Cleared roles cannot be sourced through LinkedIn or AI tools, because the candidate pool is a few thousand people nationally and filling happens through relationships built over years. Senior cyber premiums moved up 20 to 30 percent year-over-year and the trend has not peaked.

The Pricing Middle Is Disappearing
Junior IT bill rates compressed 8 to 12 percent. Senior rates expanded 15 to 30 percent. Mid-level sits flat. Your old total-cost pricing still lands in the right place on paper, but the composition inside it has shifted in a way that makes senior-heavy plans cost materially more than your model predicts. Rebuild by role level, not blended rate.

Vendor Rosters Are Getting Cut in Half
The average Q4 2025 to Q1 2026 move across our client base has been consolidating from 10 to 12 staffing vendors down to 3 or 4, and procurement is driving it because the overhead of managing a broad panel now clearly exceeds the incremental value of breadth coverage. Two or three vendors is the right number. One generalist. One specialist in your biggest gap. One optional regional partner.

What to Do About It
Three practical moves this quarter. Reprice by role level. Lead with contract-to-hire on senior roles. Cut your vendor list to three. The market is not harder than 2024. It is different.
If you want to compare notes on where your program sits against these shifts, connect or DM.

This is the MES operating system. Five departments, one data layer, fully connected. The 🤖 bots show exactly what is moving and where, in real time.

When a client call happens, the transcript automatically routes to three places: Operations gets the meeting notes and action items, HR gets the billable hours for the timesheet, and Marketing gets the raw material for this week's content. No one sends an email. No one copies a note into another system. It just moves.

When a deal closes in Sales, the signed contract kicks off a project in Operations and queues a revenue entry in Accounts. When Operations completes a project, Accounts gets the invoice trigger. When HR runs an interview, Marketing gets a transcript it can build a team spotlight from.

Everything that happens in one department becomes useful data for every other department, automatically.

What Each Street Carries:

Clients → Projects 
Client briefs, requirements, approvals, change orders, feedback

Timesheet → Invoices 
Billable hours, project time summaries, monthly billing reports

Invoices → Deals
Revenue recognition, closed deal payments, accounts receivable

Deals → Projects
Project kickoff data, signed contracts, scope of work, delivery plan

Op Calls → Content
Project updates, milestone posts, meeting summaries, progress reports

Op Calls → Timesheet
Billable time, meeting logs, task allocation, productivity data

Sales Calls → Content
Client testimonials, case studies, win highlights, partnerships

Hiring → Content
Interview transcripts, team spotlights, culture posts, recruitment content

Content → Social + Website
LinkedIn posts, newsletters, carousels, blog articles, SEO pages

--------------------

How We Built This?

Before we wrote a single line of code, we mapped every connection on that diagram by hand. Which department needs what from which other department. Where data is created and where it needs to go. Where it was dying in a handoff instead of flowing through automatically.

That map became the blueprint. Then we built it: a custom full-stack application using Claude Code covering HR, time tracking, KPIs, content generation, report writing, and CRM, with AI agent workflows running across every connection.

Working system in ten days. Updates in minutes, not quarters. No platform sprawl. One operating system. One data layer. The AI runs on the infrastructure. The infrastructure is what does the work.

Before → 8 hrs → Senior staff time compiling one CEO report from four disconnected systems

After  → Instant → One button. Live data. Top five insights from across the firm. Nothing filtered out.

The difference: Manual handoffs between departments in the connected system

--------------------

What is the biggest operating system bottleneck inside your engineering firm right now?

If you want to talk through how we approached the mapping process, or what we built on top of it, reach out directly.

This is the MES operating system. Five departments, one data layer, fully connected. The 🤖 bots show exactly what is moving and where, in real time.

When a client call happens, the transcript automatically routes to three places: Operations gets the meeting notes and action items, HR gets the billable hours for the timesheet, and Marketing gets the raw material for this week's content. No one sends an email. No one copies a note into another system. It just moves.

When a deal closes in Sales, the signed contract kicks off a project in Operations and queues a revenue entry in Accounts. When Operations completes a project, Accounts gets the invoice trigger. When HR runs an interview, Marketing gets a transcript it can build a team spotlight from.

Everything that happens in one department becomes useful data for every other department, automatically.

What Each Street Carries:

Clients → Projects 
Client briefs, requirements, approvals, change orders, feedback

Timesheet → Invoices 
Billable hours, project time summaries, monthly billing reports

Invoices → Deals
Revenue recognition, closed deal payments, accounts receivable

Deals → Projects
Project kickoff data, signed contracts, scope of work, delivery plan

Op Calls → Content
Project updates, milestone posts, meeting summaries, progress reports

Op Calls → Timesheet
Billable time, meeting logs, task allocation, productivity data

Sales Calls → Content
Client testimonials, case studies, win highlights, partnerships

Hiring → Content
Interview transcripts, team spotlights, culture posts, recruitment content

Content → Social + Website
LinkedIn posts, newsletters, carousels, blog articles, SEO pages

--------------------

How We Built This?

Before we wrote a single line of code, we mapped every connection on that diagram by hand. Which department needs what from which other department. Where data is created and where it needs to go. Where it was dying in a handoff instead of flowing through automatically.

That map became the blueprint. Then we built it: a custom full-stack application using Claude Code covering HR, time tracking, KPIs, content generation, report writing, and CRM, with AI agent workflows running across every connection.

Working system in ten days. Updates in minutes, not quarters. No platform sprawl. One operating system. One data layer. The AI runs on the infrastructure. The infrastructure is what does the work.

Before → 8 hrs → Senior staff time compiling one CEO report from four disconnected systems

After  → Instant → One button. Live data. Top five insights from across the firm. Nothing filtered out.

The difference: Manual handoffs between departments in the connected system

--------------------

What is the biggest operating system bottleneck inside your engineering firm right now?

If you want to talk through how we approached the mapping process, or what we built on top of it, reach out directly.

NASA Force: Rebuilding Talent for a New Space Era - by Frederic Eger - On April 17, 2026, NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman unveiled NASA Force, a bold initiative to inject top engineering and tech talent into the agency amid a self-proclaimed "Golden Age" of exploration. Partnering with the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) under Director Scott Kupor, this program offers 1–2 year term appointments—extendable—for early- to mid-career innovators in human spaceflight, aeronautics, and scientific discovery. Applications open immediately via USAJobs and NASA’s dedicated NASA Force portal, targeting roles to "strengthen technical depth" for Artemis lunar returns and Mars ambitions.
This move addresses chronic critiques of NASA’s talent erosion. Post-Apollo, bureaucratic inertia and risk-averse culture drove expertise to private firms like SpaceX and Blue Origin, where engineers solve real-time problems unencumbered by federal hiring freezes. Isaacman, a billionaire astronaut-entrepreneur who commanded Inspiration4 and Polaris Dawn, frames NASA Force as a "winning playbook" redux—echoing 1960s Apollo urgency when industry exchanges rebuilt civil service competencies. Term limits sidestep civil service permanence, enabling rapid influx of aerospace, software, and systems specialists while offering NASA staff industry rotations for cross-pollination.
Yet hard questions loom. Will short-term hires—mentored by industry partners—truly restore "core competencies," or merely paper over decades of underinvestment? U.S. SLS/Orion overruns exceed $25 billion; can 1–2 year stints fix systemic delays plaguing Artemis III (now late 2027)? Critics like Lori Garver argue talent alone ignores misaligned incentives—NASA’s cost-plus contracts reward bloat, not agility. Moreover, OPM’s involvement risks politicization; as Trump 2.0 prioritizes "America First," will selections favor loyalty over merit?
Strategically, NASA Force counters China’s ILRS lunar push and ESA’s Ariane 6 resurgence. By tapping Dynamism Summit networks (a16z-backed), Isaacman leverages Silicon Valley’s engineering surplus—AI, propulsion, robotics—to sustain U.S. primacy. Success metrics? Accelerated Gateway assembly, Starship HLS integration, nuclear thermal propulsion demos. Failure invites ridicule: another jobs program amid $800B Space Force budgets siphoning civilian focus.
Isaacman’s vision demands scrutiny. NASA must publish hiring rubrics, track mission impacts, and audit OPM influence. Without transparency, NASA Force risks becoming symbolic HR theater in a multiplanetary race demanding substance over slogans.

Why Job Fairs Are Losing Their Spark (And What’s Replacing Them)

There was a time when a Job Fair (Targi Pracy) felt like a "golden ticket." You’d dress up, print twenty copies of your CV on high-quality paper, and walk into a hall filled with the electric possibility of a handshake leading directly to an interview.

But let’s be honest: when was the last time you—or anyone you know—actually got hired because of a booth and a free branded pen?

The "QR Code" Trap
The biggest shift is technological. Historically, the value of a job fair was the direct line to a human being. Today, even after a great 5-minute conversation at a stand, the recruiter’s response is almost always:
"That’s great! Please scan this QR code and upload your profile to our portal."

The moment you do that, you aren't a "candidate with a face" anymore; you are a data point in an ATS (Applicant Tracking System), just like the thousands of people who applied from their couches. The human element, which used to be the USP (Unique Selling Proposition) of job fairs, has been automated out of the process.

The Rise of the "Hidden Job Market"
Data from sources like Forbes and LinkedIn consistently show that between 70% to 85% of jobs are never even advertised publicly—let alone brought to a job fair.
Companies today prioritize:
 Internal Referrals: It’s cheaper and more reliable.
 Direct Sourcing: Recruiters use AI-driven tools to find you on LinkedIn before you even know you're looking.

In this climate, a booth at a fair often serves more as an Employer Branding exercise (marketing) rather than a serious recruitment drive. Companies are there to show they are "active," not necessarily to sign contracts.

Efficiency vs. Tradition
From a candidate's perspective, the ROI (Return on Investment) of a job fair has plummeted:

4 hours of commuting and queuing vs. 4 hours of targeted networking or skill-building.
 You meet HR generalists or event staff, rarely the Hiring Managers who actually make the decisions.

 What works in 2026?
If the "Targi" format is fading, where should our energy go?
 1. Specialized meetups or hackathons where your skills are visible in real-time.
 2. Building meaningful connections on LinkedIn with people in your specific niche.
 3. Showing what you know by sharing insights, rather than just saying what you can do.
The verdict? Job fairs aren't dead, but their purpose has changed. They are great for students to learn about companies, but for the experienced professional, the "fair" has moved online and into private networks.

Recruiters—do you still find your "star" candidates at these events?

Candidates—have you ever landed a dream role through a job fair, or is it just a quest for free merch?

NASA Force: Rebuilding Talent for a New Space Era - by Frederic Eger - Video credit: NASA - On April 17, 2026, NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman unveiled NASA Force, a bold initiative to inject top engineering and tech talent into the agency amid a self-proclaimed "Golden Age" of exploration. Partnering with the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) under Director Scott Kupor, this program offers 1–2 year term appointments—extendable—for early- to mid-career innovators in human spaceflight, aeronautics, and scientific discovery. Applications open immediately via USAJobs and NASA’s dedicated NASA Force portal (https://lnkd.in/dJD6YVVY), targeting roles to "strengthen technical depth" for Artemis lunar returns and Mars ambitions.
This move addresses chronic critiques of NASA’s talent erosion. Post-Apollo, bureaucratic inertia and risk-averse culture drove expertise to private firms like SpaceX and Blue Origin, where engineers solve real-time problems unencumbered by federal hiring freezes. Isaacman, a billionaire astronaut-entrepreneur who commanded Inspiration4 and Polaris Dawn, frames NASA Force as a "winning playbook" redux—echoing 1960s Apollo urgency when industry exchanges rebuilt civil service competencies. Term limits sidestep civil service permanence, enabling rapid influx of aerospace, software, and systems specialists while offering NASA staff industry rotations for cross-pollination.
Yet hard questions loom. Will short-term hires—mentored by industry partners—truly restore "core competencies," or merely paper over decades of underinvestment? U.S. SLS/Orion overruns exceed $25 billion; can 1–2 year stints fix systemic delays plaguing Artemis III (now late 2027)? Critics like Lori Garver argue talent alone ignores misaligned incentives—NASA’s cost-plus contracts reward bloat, not agility. Moreover, OPM’s involvement risks politicization; as Trump 2.0 prioritizes "America First," will selections favor loyalty over merit?
Strategically, NASA Force counters China’s ILRS lunar push and ESA’s Ariane 6 resurgence. By tapping Dynamism Summit networks (a16z-backed), Isaacman leverages Silicon Valley’s engineering surplus—AI, propulsion, robotics—to sustain U.S. primacy. Success metrics? Accelerated Gateway assembly, Starship HLS integration, nuclear thermal propulsion demos. Failure invites ridicule: another jobs program amid $800B Space Force budgets siphoning civilian focus.
Isaacman’s vision demands scrutiny. NASA must publish hiring rubrics, track mission impacts, and audit OPM influence. Without transparency, NASA Force risks becoming symbolic HR theater in a multiplanetary race demanding substance over slogans.
